title: Performance Science2
text:
Deutsch 


  English
Hi! Ich bin Philip - Sportwissenschaftler, Trainer und leidenschaftlicher Sportler. Ich helfe Athlet*innen dabei, ihre Ziele zu erreichen - egal ob im Triathlon, Radsport oder Laufen.

title: Performance Science2
text:
Blog
Der Blog ist das Herzstück meiner Website. Hier teile ich Einblicke in wissenschaftliche Themen, Trainingsratschläge und vieles mehr (auf English).

Zum Blog

title: Performance Science2
text:
Coaching
Egal, welches Ziel du verfolgst - ich erstelle maßgeschneiderte Trainingspläne und biete individuelle Betreuung, damit du dein Ziel erreichst.
Ob erster Triathlon, persönliche Marathon-Bestzeit oder der Ötztaler Radmarathon – ich begleite dich Schritt für Schritt.

Mehr erfahren

title: Performance Science2
text:

Leidenschaft trifft auf Expertise. Ich kombiniere praktische Coaching-Erfahrung mit wissenschaftlicher Kompetenz:

M.Sc. High Performance Sport
C-Trainer Lizenz Triathlon

Lern mich kennen

Learn more

title: Über mich
text:
Ich bin Philip, Coach, Sportwissenschaftler und jemand, der Sport wirklich lebt. Ob beim Triathlontraining, auf einer Bikepacking-Tour durch die Berge oder beim Wildwasser-Kajakfahren - Bewegung ist nicht nur ein Hobby, sondern ein zentraler Teil meines Lebens.
In meiner Forschung als Sportwissenschaftler beschäfitge ich mich mit Ermüdung und dem Einsatz moderner Wearables im Ausdauertraining, etwa zur Messung der Muskelsauer-stoffsättigung. Diese wissenschaftliche Perspektive kombiniere ich in meiner Arbeit mit langjähriger Praxiserfahrung.
Schon mit 14 Jahren stand ich das erste Mal als Schwimmtrainer am Beckenrand. Seitdem durfte ich Athlet*innen in ganz unterschiedlichen Bereichen begleiten: Vom Kinderschwimmen über Nachwuchs- und Profi-Triathlon bis hin zum Kajak Guide.
Diese Vielfalt prägt meine Arbeit - und genau das gebe ich im Coaching weiter.

title: Über mich
text:
Meine Philosophie
Sport muss vor allem Spaß machen. Ich arbeite gerne sehr ambitioniert und optimiere die letzten Details, um das Beste herauszuholen - aber das ist keine Vorausetzung für meine Athlet*innen. Wenn du Freude an dem hast, was du tust - ganz egal deiner sportlichen Ziele - führt das automatisch zu persönlichem Erfolg.
Deshalb begleite ich Sportler*innen mit ganz unterschiedlichen Zielen. Jede Situation erfordert eine individuelle Herangehensweise – und genau diese Erfahrung bringe ich in mein Coaching ein.

title: Über mich
text:
Wissenschaftliche Arbeit
Als Doktorstudent in der Sportwissenschaft (University of Rome “Foro Italico”) habe ich mich auf Ausdauersport, Leistungsphysiologie- und diagnostik spezialisiert.
Durch mehrere eigene Forschungsprojekte, ein Forschungspraktikum bei Prof. Stephen Seiler - einem der führenden Experten im Ausdauersport - sowie weitere internationale Kooperationen bin ich bestens vernetzt.
Meine Arbeit fokussiert sich darauf, neue Methoden in Diagnostik, Trainingssteuerung und Trainingsmethodik zu entwickeln. Dabei ist mir die Übertragung von wissenschaftlichen Erkenntnissen in die Praxis besonders wichtig.

title: Über mich
text:
Qualifikationen

M.Sc. High-Performance Sport, Universität des Saarlandes
C-Lizenz Triathlon (Leistungssport)
B-Lizenz Fitnesstrainer
DLRG Ausbilder Schwimmen & Rettungsschwimmen

title: Über mich
text: Sportliche Momente

title: Coaching
text:
Leistungen


Trainingsplanung
Individuelle Trainingspläne für dein Ziel - egal ob Laufen, Radfahren oder Triathlon. Ich begleite dich kontinuierlich und passe das Training flexibel an dein Leben und deine Fortschritte an.


Schwimm-Coaching
Techniktraining im Schwimmen - auf Wunsch mit Videoanalyse. Gemeinsam optimieren wir deinen Schwimmstil und machen dich effizienter im Wasser.


Leistungsdiagnostik
Bestimme deine Trainingsbereiche und Leistungsfähigkeit - remote oder vor Ort. Mit dem für dich passenden Test bekommst du präzise Werte, um dein Training optimal zu steuern und Fortschritte zu messen.


Bikefitting
Deine Sitzposition entscheidet über Komfort und Leistung. Wir analysieren und optimieren dein Setup, damit du effizient und beschwerdefrei trainieren kannst.


Experten-Beratung
Ob Training, Schlaf oder Erholung – in einem 30- oder 60-minütigen Gespräch klären wir deine Fragen und finden Lösungen, die dich weiterbringen.

title: Coaching
text:
Pakete und Leistungen


“Essential”
100€ pro Monat
 Monatliche Trainingsplanung
 Monatlicher Video- oder Telefon-Call
 Unbegrenzte Kommunikation über Trainingsplattform
 Trainingsanalyse und Fortschrittskontrolle


“Premium”
200€ pro Monat
 Wöchentliche Trainingsplanung
 Wöchentliche Calls, dynamische Plananpassung
 Unbegrenzte Kommunikation über Trainingsplattform
 Individuelle Trainingsanalyse und Fortschrittskontrolle


Weitere Leistungen
Preis auf Anfrage
 Schwimm-Coaching
 Leistungsdiagnostik
 Bikefitting
 Individuelle Beratung

title: Coaching
text:
Kontaktiere Mich
Bereit dein Training aufs nächste Level zu bringen?
Dann lass uns gemeinsam herausfinden, wie ich dich am besten unterstützen kann.
Buche ein kostenloses Erstgespräch, und wir besprechen deine Ziele, dein aktuelles Training und die nächsten Schritte.

title: Blog
text:
As scientists we are doing our own little research, creating new knowledge and presenting it to our peers. With this blog I am trying to take the next step: Provide research insights and communicate scientific ideas to a broader audience.
Topics will range from research article discussions, opinion pieces and coaching thoughts.






























































What is scientific training - and does it exist?


Repeatedly athletes and coaches claim to follow a scientific training regime - why this probably is no the case.





10.02.2026


Philip Skotzke





No matching items

title: What is scientific training - and does it exist?
text: Hi, this is Philip on Performance Science2, and this is my first blog post. Have some patience with my language not being as sharp as it could be. I’m expecting (hoping) to get better! In this first blog post I will dive into the idea of scientific training - and why it’s often a misperceived.

title: What is scientific training - and does it exist?
text:
The case

In 2023, when the “Norwegian Hype Train” was moving fast, winning all major races on the way, a young Australian brought the train to an abrupt stop - at least for this moment. Max Neumann won the men’s PTO European Open race and didn’t stop at the finish line. In the post race interview he was shooting against the Norwegians, and attributed his victory to his “no bullshit science” approach (video ref), receiving wide applause. Indeed, the Norwegians are (were) famous for being equipped with dozens of sensor-technology and giving hundreds of drops of blood to measure blood lactate concentration each year. Often this extensive support - only possible with more people involved to analyze the data - is characterized as scientific training.

Maybe the Norwegians never claimed themselves to base their training on science, but the quote from Max Neumann in the post race interview reflects a very common notion: There would be something like scientific training.
Let’s stop here and take a step back. What does science even mean? And does scientific training even exist?

title: What is scientific training - and does it exist?
text:
What is science?
You may be surprised to read that even scientists don’t agree on a definition of science. Characetistics found in many definitions are:

Science is a system. It is structured in organizations that follow common practices (“rituals”)
Science aims to generate generalizable knowledge (“truths”)
Science tests hypotheses generated from prior knowledge
Science uses specialized methods

There are some parallels with what in sports generally is considered scientific training:

Technology use - While some technology is well-established (e.g heart rate sensors and power meters), novel devices such as core temperature sensors (and pills) or muscle oxygenation monitors are becoming used more frequently. This trend originates in technology transfer and democratization of knowledge.
Personalized insights - technology is used to understand the athlete’s response to training and how they recover in real world conditions.
Precise training control - the insights are then used to execute training sessions and maximize adaptation potential while minimizing recovery needs.

Next to the similarities there are some relevant differences:
Training usually does not aim to generate knowledge applicable beyond a single athlete. When done right, technology can be used to understand how one athlete handles a training session. However, training is performed in varying environments (e.g. hot summer day versus mild spring), complicating generalizability. If we acknowledge that athletes respond differently to training, it becomes clear that it is hard to generate new knowledge.
The final point is about hypothesis testing. Smart coaches and athletes can use technology to test if a new training method resulted in performance gains. There is one problem though: In science, experimental conditions are strictly controlled to test the cause-effect of a single variable. This is unrealistic with real athletes. When preparing for a competition many aspects are changed at once: Nutrition is optimized, different intervals are performed while doing a training block in altitude. Consequently, the athlete will know the combination of all these things leads to a good performance but it is impossible to quantify individual contributions.
As we can see, training can approximate and mimic scientific principles. This assumes that the coach-athlete team really is meaningfully using the data to form hypothesis on training responses and critically evaluates them to create an explicit understanding on the athlete’s training response. From my experience all the tech typically is used to create an implicit understanding of an athlete, their strengths, weaknesses and response characteristics. Yet, it is in the core of science to clearly understand cause-effect relationships. Therefore, I would argue that all the fancy-looking social media content and coaches boasting about being scientific is about one thing: Marketing

title: What is scientific training - and does it exist?
text:
Evidence-based vs. Evidence-informed training
As mentioned above, science aims to generate general truths. Most research in sport science is conducted to be applicable and guide decision making. The knowledge generated is extensively democratized and leads to drastically improved training programs, better nutrition strategies and understanding of recovery compared to 20 years ago.
When translating scienctific knowledge and applying it in the work with athletes, there is one important aspect to consider: Due to the nature of science it can tell us what will work for most athletes but it doesn’t tell if it the best for one specific athlete. And it does this in a very specific way.
Let’s examplify this: We read a study comparing different
There is a second dimension I didn’t touch on so far. I believe many people claiming to do scientific training are saying their training is evidence-based. But what does that even mean? Read a study comparing different HIT interval protocols and apply the best study? If you are doing this, you’re fallen for a trap: Research use extreme protocols to maximize the primary variance between different protocols. If you base your training on extreme levels, you don’t apply the scientific knowledge, you’re simply replicating what researchers thought would be good to test their hypothesis.
A few years ago X&Y have published a scientific article in journal x (ref), highlighting this misconception. They propose the term evidence-informed as sensible alternative. It puts the athlete, coach or practitioner into the position to synthesize a broad range of scientific literature and then take out the specific aspects they deem relevant for a specific athlete in a specific situation.
I will challenge any coach who claims they provide scientific training or even evidence-based training. Creating an evidence-informed system takes time.

title: What is scientific training - and does it exist?
text:
Motivation

In 2023, when the “Norwegian Hype Train” was moving fast, winning all major triathlon races, a young Australian brought the train to an abrupt halt - at least for a short moment. Max Neumann from Australia won the men’s PTO European Open race and didn’t stop at the finish line. In the post race interview he was shooting against the Norwegians, and attributed his victory to his “no bullshit science” approach (video), receiving wide applause. Indeed, the Norwegian triathletes are famous for training equipped with dozens of sensor-technology and measuring lactate constantly. Often this extensive monitoring program is characterized as scientific training.

Maybe the Norwegians never claimed themselves to do scientific training but the quote from Max Neumann echos a common notion: There is something like scientific training.
Let’s stop here and take a step back. What does science even mean? Does scientific training exist?

Google Analytics

Diese Website benutzt Google Analytics, einen Webanalysedienst der Google Inc. (‘’Google’‘). Google Analytics verwendet sog.’‘Cookies’’, Textdateien, die auf Ihrem Computer gespeichert werden und die eine Analyse der Benutzung der Website durch Sie ermöglicht. Die durch den Cookie erzeugten Informationen über Ihre Benutzung dieser Website (einschließlich Ihrer IP-Adresse) wird an einen Server von Google in den USA übertragen und dort gespeichert. Sie können die Installation der Cookies durch eine entsprechende Einstellung Ihrer Browser Software verhindern; wir weisen Sie jedoch darauf hin, dass Sie in diesem Fall gegebenenfalls nicht sämtliche Funktionen dieser Website voll umfänglich nutzen können. title: What is scientific training - and does it exist?
text:
The false claim
So far we discovered that science is defined by specific characteristics that will differ vastly from working with individual athletes. Both worlds exist in paralell and can learn from each other. The scientist looks at what athletes are doing and tries to understand it. The coach takes knowledge and applies it to a training program. They form a strong synergy.
But why do coaches claim to b.
We discovered that there is no scientific training. Still, we can leverage science in the training process. This is typically referred to as evidence-based or evidence-informed training.
